title: Myocarditis
body_html:
Definition
Myocarditis refers to inflammation of the myocardium. It may be acute or chronic, and can mimic myocardial infarction with chest pain and ECG changes, but is distinguished by non-ischemic injury.

Click on image to enlarge

Epidemiology
True incidence is difficult to gauge as the condition is often underdiagnosed. In England, myocardial inflammation accounted for ~0.04% of hospital admissions.

More common in Men with male to female ratio of 1.5:1

Aetiology
Infectious causes (most common):
  • Viruses: Coxsackievirus, adenovirus, parvovirus B19, enteroviruses, Civid-19, HIV, EBV, hepatitis viruses
  • Bacteria: e.g., diphtheria
  • Parasites, fungi, spirochetes (in endemic or immunocompromised patients)
Immune / inflammatory causes:
  • Autoimmune conditions (SLE, sarcoidosis)
  • Hypersensitivity / eosinophilic myocarditis
  • Giant cell myocarditis
Toxic / drug-induced / miscellaneous:
  • Drugs: anthracyclines, cocaine, cytotoxic agents
  • Radiation, heavy metals, chemicals, electric shock, hyperthermia

  • Coxsackie virus is the most common viral cause of myocarditis in the UK, Europe and the USA
  • Worldwide Diptheria is the most common bacterial cause

  • Lithium, cocaine and several chemotherapy agents may cause myocarditis via a direct cytotoxic effect.
  • Several drugs may also cause myocarditis via hypersensitivity reactions.

Clinical features
There is a wide spectrum of presentations from asymptomatic to fulminant myocarditis with rapid cardiac failure.

Symptoms include:
  • Fatigue
  • Chest pain (~35%)
  • Dyspnoea
  • Palpitations (arrhythmias)
  • Fever
Examination findings include:
  • Heart failure signs (oedema, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea etc)
  • Soft S1, S4 gallop

Investigations


Investigation		Findings
ECGNon-specific abnormalities: ST elevation/depression, T wave inversion, AV block, supraventricular arrhythmias
Blood testsElevated troponin, CK; inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR); full blood count, renal & liver panels
CXRCardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion (if heart failure)
Cardiac MRITissue inflammation, edema, scar; differentiates myocarditis from infarction
Endomyocardial biopsyGold standard; definitive histology; used in selected cases


Differential diagnoses
  • Acute coronary syndrome (MI)
  • Congestive heart failure from other causes
  • Pulmonary edema / pneumonia
  • Other chest pain causes (PE, pericarditis)

Management
  • 1. Supportive therapy: inotropic support, heart failure management
  • 2. Severe cases: ICU admission, mechanical circulatory support if needed
  • 3. Treat underlying cause: antivirals, antimicrobials if applicable
  • 4. Immunosuppression / steroids / IVIG: only in specific cases (giant cell, eosinophilic myocarditis)
  • 5. Anticoagulation: if thromboembolism risk is high (AF, ventricular thrombus)
  • 6. Restrict physical activity post-acute phase for myocardial recovery

Complications
  • Dilated cardiomyopathy and chronic heart failure
  • Cardiogenic shock
  • Arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death
  • Recurrent myocarditis

Prognosis
Prognosis is variable ranging from complete recovery to chronic heart failure or death

Prognostic factors: left ventricular dysfunction, hemodynamic compromise, arrhythmias, myocardial inflammation/scarring

Summary

Feature		Key Points
DefinitionInflammation of myocardium; may mimic MI
Common CausesViral infections, autoimmune, drugs/toxins
PresentationFatigue, chest pain, dyspnoea, palpitations, fever
InvestigationsECG, troponin, CXR, cardiac MRI, biopsy
TreatmentSupportive, treat underlying cause, ICU if severe
ComplicationsHeart failure, cardiogenic shock, arrhythmias, recurrent myocarditis
PrognosisHighly variable; depends on LV function and severity
NCBI00
Update on Myocarditis: From Etiology and Clinical Picture to Modern Diagnostics and Methods of Treatment
